{{short description|none}}
{{Use British English|date=October 2025}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=October 2025}}

{{MedalCompetition|[[COSAFA Cup]]}}
{{MedalSilver|[[2002 COSAFA Cup|2002 Southern Africa]]|}}
{{MedalSilver|[[2003 COSAFA Cup|2003 Southern Africa]]|}}
{{MedalCompetition|[[CECAFA Cup]]}}
{{MedalGold|[[1978 CECAFA Cup|1978 Malawi]]|}}
{{MedalGold|[[1979 CECAFA Cup|1979 Kenya]]|}}
{{MedalGold|[[1988 CECAFA Cup|1988 Malawi]]|}}
{{MedalSilver|[[1975 CECAFA Cup|1975 Zambia]]|}}
{{MedalSilver|[[1984 CECAFA Cup|1984 Uganda]]|}}
{{MedalSilver|[[1989 CECAFA Cup|1989 Kenya]]|}}
{{MedalBronze|[[1977 CECAFA Cup|1977 Somalia]]|}}
{{MedalBronze|[[1980 CECAFA Cup|1980 Sudan]]|}}
{{MedalBronze|[[1985 CECAFA Cup|1985 Zimbabwe]]|}}|shorts3=ff0000}}

The '''Malawi national football team''' ({{langx|ny|Timu ya dziko la Malawi}}) represents [[Malawi]] in [[association football]] and is controlled by the [[Football Association of Malawi]]. Before 1966, it was known as the '''Nyasaland national football team'''.

Known as the '''Flames''', Malawi has qualified for three [[Africa Cup of Nations]], in [[1984 Africa Cup of Nations|1984]], [[2010 Africa Cup of Nations|2010]] and [[2021 Africa Cup of Nations|2021]]. They also won the [[CECAFA Cup]] in [[1978 CECAFA Cup|1978]], [[1979 CECAFA Cup|1979]] and [[1988 CECAFA Cup|1988]] and finished fourth in the [[2024 Four Nations Football Tournament]].

==History==
===Coaches===
The first full-time coach of the Malawi national football team was Ron Meades. The [[British Council]] arranged for his appointment through the [[English Football Association]] to coach the team for the Republic Celebrations, playing [[Madagascar national football team|Madagascar]] and [[Zambia national football team|Zambia]]. [[Ray Batchelor]] was coach for four years from 11 November 1967 to 10 October 1971, guiding the team in 23 [[friendly match]]es and two [[Olympic Games]]. Players during this time included  future coaches in [[Henry Moyo (football)|Henry Moyo]], Brian Griffin, and Yasin Osman. Other coaches included Brazilian Wander Moreira, British [[Ted Powell]], and the first locally-born coach, Alex Masanjala, who was appointed as an interim before [[Henry Moyo (football)|Henry Moyo]]. Moyo was the first former national player to coach the Flames, and stayed in the position for five years. As of January 2019, only nine coaches had been in charge for more than 20 games. The most successful coaches were Powell, Moyo, and [[Kim Splidsboel]] (2000–2002) from Denmark.<ref>{{cite web | title=Throw Back Thursday with Mario Malawi Coaches Records... Compiled by Louis Alec Mario Antoine|date=17 January 2019 | website=Facebook | url=https://www.facebook.com/FaMalawi/photos/throw-back-thursday-with-mariomalawi-coaches-records-in-1962-the-flames-malawi-u/2243008332390018/ | access-date=4 January 2024}}</ref><ref>{{cite book | last=Antoine | first=M. | title=A History of Nyasaland and Malawi Football: Volume 1 1935 to 1969 | publisher=AuthorHouse UK | year=2022 | isbn=978-1-6655-9839-2 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=G5lyEAAAQBAJ&pg=RA1-PA2 | access-date=4 January 2024 | page=1-PA2}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | title=Splidsboel heads home | website=BBC SPORT | date=27 November 2002 | url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/africa/2519635.stm | access-date=4 January 2024}}</ref>

===Tournaments===
Malawi first qualified for an [[African Cup of Nations]] in [[1984 African Cup of Nations|1984]], when only eight teams competed in the tournament in Ivory Coast. After a 3-0 defeat against [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]], World Cup participants in 1982 and 1986, the Malawi drew 2-2 in the second game against eventual finalists [[Nigeria national football team|Nigeria]]. With a 0–1 loss against [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]], Malawi ended the group stage with 1 point at the bottom of the table and was eliminated from the tournament.{{citation needed|date=January 2024}}

After 26 years, Malawi took part in an [[Africa Cup of Nations]] again in [[2010 Africa Cup of Nations|2010]]. The team benefited from the fact that the continental elimination round for the [[2010 FIFA World Cup]] also served as the qualifying round for AFCON. In the group matches of the third round, Malawi, third place behind [[Côte d'Ivoire national football team|Côte d'Ivoire]] and [[Burkina Faso national football team|Burkina Faso]] and with a record of only four points and 4–11 goal difference, was enough to make it to the continental finals.{{citation needed|date=January 2024}}

At the tournament in Angola, [[Kinnah Phiri]]'s team caused a big surprise. In the first group game, Malawi defeated [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]], which had qualified for the World Cup, 3-0. One of the goalscorers was striker [[Russel Mwafulirwa]], who at the time was under contract for the Swedish first division club [[IFK Norrköping]] and was thus one of only two players in the 23-man squad active in Europe. Malawi lost the second game against hosts [[Angola national football team|Angola]], 2-0.{{citation needed|date=January 2024}}

For Malawi to have reached the quarter-finals for the first time, a draw in the last group game would have been enough. However, the team lost 3-1 to [[Mali national football team|Mali]]. After just three minutes, the Malawians were 2-0 down, a goal from [[Russel Mwafulirwa|Mwafulirwa]] did nothing to avert the team's elimination.{{citation needed|date=January 2024}}

==Results and fixtures==
The following is a list of match results in the last 12 months, as well as any future matches that have been scheduled.

===Coaching history===

:Caretaker coaches are listed in ''italics''
{{div col|colwidth=27em}}
*{{flagicon|MWI}} [[Jack Chamangwana]] (1998–99)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} [[Young Chimodzi]] (1999–00)
*{{flagicon|DEN}} [[Kim Splidsboel]] (2000–02)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} ''[[Alan Gillett (football)|Alan Gillett]]'' (2003)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} ''[[Edington Ng'onamo]]'' (2003–04)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} [[John Kaputa]] (2004)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} [[Yassin Osman]] (2004–05)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} ''[[Mike Hennigan (footballer)|Michael Hennigan]]'' (2005)
*{{flagicon|GER}} [[Burkhard Ziese]] (2005–06)
*{{flagicon|MWI|2010}} ''[[Kinnah Phiri]]'' (2006–07)
*{{flagicon|ENG}} [[Stephen Constantine]] (2007–08)
*{{flagicon|MWI|2010}} [[Kinnah Phiri]] (2009–12)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} ''[[Edington Ng'onamo]]'' (2012–13)
*{{flagicon|BEL}} ''[[Tom Saintfiet]]'' (2013)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} [[Young Chimodzi]] (2014–15)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} ''[[Ernest Mtawali]]'' (2015–16)
*{{flagicon|BDI}} ''[[Ramadhan Nsanzurwimo]]'' (2016–17)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} ''[[Gerald Phiri Sr.]]'' (2017)
*{{flagicon|BEL}} [[Ronny Van Geneugden]] (2017–19)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} [[Meke Mwase]] (2019–2021)
*{{flagicon|ROM}} [[Mario Marinică]] (2021–2023)
*{{flagicon|MWI}} [[Patrick Mabedi]] (2023–2024)
*{{flagicon|ZIM}} ''[[Kalisto Pasuwa]]'' (2024–present)
{{div col end}}

==Players==

===Current squad===
The following players were named to the squad for the Mukuru 4 Nations Tournament friendly matches against [[Zambia national football team|Zambia]] and [[Botswana national football team|Botswana]] on 28 and 31 March 2026.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://fam.mw/pasuwa-names-final-flames-squad-for-four-nations-tournament/|title=Pasuwa Names Final Flames Squad for Four Nations Tournament|publisher=[[Football Association of Malawi]]|date=24 March 2026}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://fam.mw/pasuwa-names-23-local-players-for-flames-camp-ahead-of-four-nations-tournament/|title=Pasuwa Names 23 Local Players for Flames Camp Ahead of Four Nations Tournament|publisher=Football Association of Malawi|date=5 March 2026}}</ref>

''Caps and goals correct as of 31 March 2026, after the match against [[Botswana national football team|Botswana]].''

{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[George Chikooka]]|age={{birth date and age|1997|4|4|df=y}}|caps=15|goals=0|club=[[Silver Strikers FC|Silver Strikers]]|clubnat=MWI}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[William Thole]]|age={{birth date and age|1998|10|2|df=y}}|caps=13|goals=0|club=[[Simba Bhora F.C.|Simba Bhora]]|clubnat=ZIM}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Joshua Waka]]|age={{birth date and age|2004|7|24|df=y}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[Ekhaya FC|Ekhaya]]|clubnat=MWI}}
{{nat fs break}}

{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=FW|name=[[Ephraim Kondowe]]|age={{birth date and age|2002|1|2|df=y}}|caps=3|goals=0|club=[[Nyasa Big Bullets FC|Nyasa Big Bullets]]|clubnat=MWI}}
{{nat fs end}}

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have been called up in 12 months preceding the above draft.
{{nat fs r start}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Dalitso Khungwa]]|age={{birth date and age|1999|8|7|df=y}}|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[Mighty Wanderers FC|Mighty Wanderers]]|clubnat=MWI|latest=Mukuru 4 Nations Tournament <sup>PRE</sup>}}

==Records==
{{Updated|8 September 2025}}<ref name="RSSSF">{{cite web |last1=Mamrud |first1=Roberto |title=Malawi – Record International Players |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/malaw-recintlp.html |website=RSSSF |access-date=2 February 2023 |archive-date=24 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230324222306/https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/malaw-recintlp.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Malawi.''

==Head-to-head record==
''As of 9 June 2026 after match against [[Ethiopia national football team|Ethiopia]]''<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.eloratings.net/Malawi.htm|title=World Football Elo Ratings: Malawi|publisher=Elo Ratings|access-date=15 July 2017|archive-date=18 July 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170718055645/http://www.eloratings.net/Malawi.htm|url-status=live}}</ref>

==Honours==
===Continental===
* '''[[Football at the African Games|African Games]]'''<sup>'''1'''</sup>
** {{Bronze3}} Bronze medal (1): 1987

===Regional===
* '''[[CECAFA Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (3):''' [[1978 CECAFA Cup|1978]], [[1979 CECAFA Cup|1979]], [[1988 CECAFA Cup|1988]]
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (3): [[1975 CECAFA Cup|1975]], [[1984 CECAFA Cup|1984]], [[1989 CECAFA Cup|1989]]
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (3): [[1977 CECAFA Cup|1977]], [[1980 CECAFA Cup|1980]], [[1985 CECAFA Cup|1985]]
* '''[[COSAFA Cup]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (2): [[2002 COSAFA Cup|2002]], [[2003 COSAFA Cup|2003]]

;Notes
# Competition organized by [[Association of National Olympic Committees of Africa|ANOCA]], officially not recognized by FIFA.
